Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I felt like it or until something was due soon. Being 25 and having been through the university system for a while, I've learned I can cram effectively when I need to. I'd spend maybe 25 hours a week studying, but those hours were bunched up irregularly rather than spread out nicely. I'm a visual learner, so when I did study, I'd focus on diagrams, videos, and making my own sketches to understand concepts.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd be grinding for hours. I never really established a Monday-Wednesday-Friday kind of schedule or anything like that. Deadlines were my main motivator to actually sit down and focus. With my attendance being what it was, I compensated by studying intensely when I actually did engage.
Q3: Usually I'd log into the LMS when I remembered an assignment was coming up or when I got a notification from a classmate in our peer group. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since I wasn't always in class. The peer support system actually helped remind me to check in more than I would have otherwise. If someone in my study group mentioned something from the course, that would prompt me to go look.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a true routine for me. There were stretches where I'd log in more regularly because my peer group was active and we'd be discussing things. But it was more reactive than habitual. If my friends were posting in discussions, I'd jump in too. The social aspect made it feel less like a chore, but I never reached the point where I'd check it daily out of habit.
Q5: The video content was genuinely useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I watched a lot of video material, probably over two and a half hours worth. Being able to pause, rewind, and really study the visual explanations helped concepts click for me. The discussion posts were also valuable because my peers would explain things differently than the instructor, and sometimes that alternative perspective was what I needed.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading-heavy resources and some of the supplementary materials. If something was just walls of text without visuals, I'd skim it or skip it entirely. Some of the optional readings felt like they were there just to pad the course. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what actually helped me learn versus what was just extra.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to understanding core concepts or preparing for exams. If I could see how it would help my grade or genuinely teach me something useful, I'd engage with it. Visual content, practice problems, and discussions where people were actually wrestling with ideas felt valuable. Busywork or activities that seemed designed just to track participation felt pointless.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could take or leave based on my judgment. Required ones had a different weight because they affected my grade directly. I'd often skip optional stuff unless my peer group was doing it or unless I was genuinely curious. The required activities got done, even if last minute, because there were consequences. Optional ones had to earn my attention.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download videos and PDFs to study offline since my internet access isn't always reliable and I have limited data. I'd take handwritten notes, draw diagrams, and work through problems on paper. A lot of my actual learning happened with physical materials spread out on my desk, not clicking through the LMS. I also used some YouTube videos and free resources online to supplement what the course provided.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely from printed materials or downloaded content, and the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours working through concepts using my notes and diagrams, testing myself, making connections. None of that shows up anywhere. My exam score reflects that learning happened, but the system wouldn't know I was studying unless I was actively clicking around.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty common for me. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it, and then study from that file repeatedly offline. The LMS might show one view, but I'd have watched that video three or four times on my laptop without internet. It was more practical given my situation and honestly more convenient than streaming every time.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I just couldn't bring myself to engage. Usually it was when other life stuff was demanding attention or when the course material felt disconnected from anything I cared about. Being 25, I've got responsibilities outside of school that younger students might not have. Sometimes I'd look at the workload and just feel like drifting away from it for a while.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. When I had other things going on, the course would take a backseat. Fatigue was a factor too—after dealing with life stuff, logging into an LMS felt like the last thing I wanted to do. I'd compensate by being more efficient when I did engage, focusing on high-impact activities rather than everything.
Q14: Oh definitely. Sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't absorbing anything. Those sessions were usually when I was tired or stressed about other things. I'd have to go back and actually study the material properly later.
Q15: I postponed assignments regularly, even important ones. Part of it was knowing I could pull it together last minute and still do well—my exam scores prove I understand the material. Part of it was just not feeling motivated until the pressure was real. It's a bad habit I've developed over the years, but it's worked for me grade-wise even if my assignment completion rate suffers.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active—watching videos, checking resources, posting in discussions to clarify things. Without deadlines, I'd drift. The structure they provided was necessary for me because I don't have great self-imposed discipline with this stuff.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, especially when it was specific and showed me where I'd gone wrong. Good feedback made me want to engage more with similar content to improve. But honestly, I didn't always check feedback promptly. Sometimes I'd get a grade and just move on without really digging into the comments. The peer support helped here—my group would discuss feedback together.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework, but I often worked around it rather than within it. I'd focus on what was graded and what I needed for exams, sometimes skipping the intended sequence. The structure helped me know what existed, but I engaged with it on my own terms. Having clear modules and deadlines at least gave me something to anchor my sporadic efforts to.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier since it matched my learning style. Having peer discussions available also helped because I could learn from classmates. What made it harder was when there was too much text-based content or when the purpose of an activity wasn't clear. Also, some of the interface stuff was clunky, which added friction when I was already not super motivated.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is searching for understanding but struggling to find it. They might be clicking through everything trying to make sense of the material but not feeling confident enough to submit work. Or they could be dealing with anxiety about their submissions not being good enough. High activity doesn't mean productive activity.
Q21: That basically describes me. My attendance and LMS activity don't look great, but I scored really well on exams and got a distinction. I study effectively offline, I understand the material deeply when I engage with it, and I test well. The LMS just doesn't capture how I actually learn. Someone looking at my logs might think I'm barely trying, but my results say otherwise.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people log in, what they click on, and completion of tracked activities. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, discussions with peers outside the platform, and genuine understanding. They measure presence, not learning. Someone could have perfect log activity and understand nothing, while someone like me has spotty logs but solid comprehension.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting mental effort into learning it. It's not about how many times you log in or how long you watch videos—it's about whether you're genuinely thinking about the concepts and trying to master them. For me, engagement happens in bursts of intense focus rather than consistent daily activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my offline studying, my peer conversations outside the platform, or the mental effort I put into understanding concepts. It wouldn't capture that I downloaded materials and studied them repeatedly. It also wouldn't show that despite appearing disengaged by metrics, I was actually highly motivated to understand the material when it mattered. My distinction grade came from real learning that happened mostly off the radar.
